metadata.note: Weak-field Landé-g treatment scoped; 2P3/2 adjacent spacing corrected to 4/3 mu_B B; positions separated from intensities and strong-field crossover.

skin_warnings: B15: palette=claude but the outro is 'un-annotated' — OUTRO LAW wants ClaudeTitleOutro

beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Zeeman's first magnetic splitting looked like a tidy triplet. Then atoms produced crowded, uneven-looking multiplets. Physicists called them anomalous until electron spin and angular-momentum addition supplied the missing bookkeeping.

props.command: Why do weak-field Zeeman multiplets look irregular once electron spin matters?
props.segment: The Anomalous Zeeman Effect Isn't Anomalous
output: Each J level has its own Landé g factor.
Different rung spacings create an uneven transition comb.

beats.narration_text: The old orbital-only cartoon assigns magnetic shifts proportional to m sub l. Adjacent sublevels are equally spaced by one Bohr magneton times B, and simple allowed transitions can produce a symmetric triplet.

Electron spin carries its own magnetic moment, with a g factor close to two rather than the orbital value one. Orbital and spin angular momenta also couple internally to form total angular momentum J.

In the weak-field regime, spin–orbit coupling is stronger than the Zeeman perturbation. L and S remain coupled, so n, l, j, and m j label the states. The field shifts each one by g J times mu B B times m j.

The Landé g factor compresses how orbital and spin magnetic moments project along J. It depends on l, s, and j. Different fine-structure levels can therefore respond to the same field with different slopes.

For hydrogen two-P three-halves, g J is four-thirds. For two-P one-half, g J is two-thirds. Both fans have equal spacing within themselves, but the first fan's adjacent rung spacing is twice the second's.

Specifically, adjacent two-P three-halves rungs differ by four-thirds mu B B. The two-P one-half doublet has two rungs separated by two-thirds mu B B in total. Confusing those two quantities erases the contrast.

A spectrum records energy differences between an upper and lower fan. Allowed delta m j equals zero or plus-or-minus one connections sample two different g factors. Their frequency shifts need not form one evenly spaced triplet, so the combined comb looks irregular.

The g factors determine level slopes and therefore line positions. They do not by themselves determine brightness. Relative intensities require transition matrix elements, angular-momentum coefficients, populations, polarization, and experimental geometry.

The Landé formula is a weak-field result: mu B B must be small compared with the relevant fine-structure separation. In intermediate fields, Zeeman and spin–orbit terms must be diagonalized together. The lines curve through the crossover.

In the strong-field Paschen–Back limit, the external field decouples L and S. The good labels become m sub l and m sub s, and the leading shift is mu B B times m sub l plus two m sub s. The pattern reorganizes.

Your turn. At one tesla, mu B B is five-point-seven-eight-eight times ten to the minus five electron volts. Find the adjacent rung spacing for g J equals four-thirds and for g J equals two-thirds, then take their ratio.

Four-thirds gives about seven-point-seven-two times ten to the minus five electron volts. Two-thirds gives about three-point-eight-six times ten to the minus five. The ratio is exactly two in the ideal weak-field model.

The anomaly was never random spacing inside one J level. It was multiple angular-momentum manifolds, each regular on its own ruler, combined through constrained transitions. Add spin, choose the field regime, and the pattern becomes accountable.
The Anomalous Zeeman Effect Isn't Anomalous. Liam, in for Bear.
